Advanced Divide and Conquer

Overview

Divide and Conquer is a powerful algorithmic paradigm that breaks complex problems into smaller subproblems, solves them recursively, and combines the results. This approach is particularly effective for inversion counting, range queries, and merge-based operations.

Key Properties

  • Time Complexity: Typically O(n log n) for most problems
  • Space Complexity: O(n) for auxiliary arrays, O(log n) for recursion stack
  • Core Idea: Divide problem into halves, solve recursively, merge results
  • When to Use: Counting inversions, range problems, merge operations
  • Key Technique: Modified merge sort with custom merge logic

Core Characteristics

  • Divide: Split problem into smaller subproblems
  • Conquer: Solve subproblems recursively
  • Combine: Merge solutions to get final answer
  • Optimal Substructure: Problem can be broken down optimally
  • Merge Logic: Custom combine step for specific requirements

Problem Categories

Category 1: Inversion Counting

  • Description: Count pairs where left element > right element
  • Examples: LC 315 (Count Smaller After Self), LC 493 (Reverse Pairs), LC 327 (Count Range Sum)
  • Pattern: Modified merge sort with counting during merge

Category 2: Range Sum Problems

  • Description: Count elements/subarrays in specific ranges
  • Examples: LC 327 (Count of Range Sum), LC 493 (Reverse Pairs with condition)
  • Pattern: Prefix sums + divide and conquer

Category 3: Array Reconstruction

  • Description: Build arrays with specific ordering properties
  • Examples: LC 1649 (Create Sorted Array), LC 2426 (Pairs Satisfying Inequality)
  • Pattern: Merge sort with reconstruction logic

Templates & Algorithms

Template Comparison Table

Template Type Use Case Time Complexity When to Use
Basic Inversion Count Count inversions O(n log n) Simple inversion problems
Conditional Inversion Count with conditions O(n log n) Reverse pairs, range conditions
Range Query D&C Range sum counting O(n log n) Subarray sum problems
Reconstruction D&C Build sorted arrays O(n log n) Array construction problems

Template 1: Basic Inversion Counting

1def count_inversions(arr):
2    """Count total number of inversions using merge sort"""
3    def merge_and_count(arr, temp, left, mid, right):
4        i, j, k = left, mid + 1, left
5        inv_count = 0
6
7        # Merge with inversion counting
8        while i <= mid and j <= right:
9            if arr[i] <= arr[j]:
10                temp[k] = arr[i]
11                i += 1
12            else:
13                temp[k] = arr[j]
14                # Count inversions: all elements from i to mid are > arr[j]
15                inv_count += (mid - i + 1)
16                j += 1
17            k += 1
18
19        # Copy remaining elements
20        while i <= mid:
21            temp[k] = arr[i]
22            i += 1
23            k += 1
24        while j <= right:
25            temp[k] = arr[j]
26            j += 1
27            k += 1
28
29        # Copy back to original array
30        for i in range(left, right + 1):
31            arr[i] = temp[i]
32
33        return inv_count
34
35    def merge_sort_and_count(arr, temp, left, right):
36        inv_count = 0
37        if left < right:
38            mid = (left + right) // 2
39            inv_count += merge_sort_and_count(arr, temp, left, mid)
40            inv_count += merge_sort_and_count(arr, temp, mid + 1, right)
41            inv_count += merge_and_count(arr, temp, left, mid, right)
42        return inv_count
43
44    temp = [0] * len(arr)
45    return merge_sort_and_count(arr[:], temp, 0, len(arr) - 1)

Template 2: Conditional Inversion Counting

1def count_reverse_pairs(nums):
2    """Count pairs where nums[i] > 2 * nums[j] for i < j"""
3    def merge_and_count(nums, temp, left, mid, right):
4        # Count reverse pairs first (before sorting)
5        count = 0
6        j = mid + 1
7        for i in range(left, mid + 1):
8            while j <= right and nums[i] > 2 * nums[j]:
9                j += 1
10            count += j - (mid + 1)
11
12        # Now perform regular merge
13        i, j, k = left, mid + 1, left
14        while i <= mid and j <= right:
15            if nums[i] <= nums[j]:
16                temp[k] = nums[i]
17                i += 1
18            else:
19                temp[k] = nums[j]
20                j += 1
21            k += 1
22
23        while i <= mid:
24            temp[k] = nums[i]
25            i += 1
26            k += 1
27        while j <= right:
28            temp[k] = nums[j]
29            j += 1
30            k += 1
31
32        for i in range(left, right + 1):
33            nums[i] = temp[i]
34
35        return count
36
37    def merge_sort_and_count(nums, temp, left, right):
38        count = 0
39        if left < right:
40            mid = (left + right) // 2
41            count += merge_sort_and_count(nums, temp, left, mid)
42            count += merge_sort_and_count(nums, temp, mid + 1, right)
43            count += merge_and_count(nums, temp, left, mid, right)
44        return count
45
46    temp = [0] * len(nums)
47    return merge_sort_and_count(nums[:], temp, 0, len(nums) - 1)

Template 3: Range Sum Divide and Conquer

1def count_range_sum(nums, lower, upper):
2    """Count subarrays with sum in [lower, upper]"""
3    def merge_and_count(prefix_sums, temp, left, mid, right):
4        count = 0
5        j = k = mid + 1
6
7        # For each prefix sum in left half
8        for i in range(left, mid + 1):
9            # Find range [j, k) where prefix_sums[j] - prefix_sums[i] is in [lower, upper]
10            while j <= right and prefix_sums[j] - prefix_sums[i] < lower:
11                j += 1
12            while k <= right and prefix_sums[k] - prefix_sums[i] <= upper:
13                k += 1
14            count += k - j
15
16        # Regular merge
17        i, j, p = left, mid + 1, left
18        while i <= mid and j <= right:
19            if prefix_sums[i] <= prefix_sums[j]:
20                temp[p] = prefix_sums[i]
21                i += 1
22            else:
23                temp[p] = prefix_sums[j]
24                j += 1
25            p += 1
26
27        while i <= mid:
28            temp[p] = prefix_sums[i]
29            i += 1
30            p += 1
31        while j <= right:
32            temp[p] = prefix_sums[j]
33            j += 1
34            p += 1
35
36        for i in range(left, right + 1):
37            prefix_sums[i] = temp[i]
38
39        return count
40
41    def divide_and_conquer(prefix_sums, temp, left, right):
42        if left >= right:
43            return 0
44
45        mid = (left + right) // 2
46        count = divide_and_conquer(prefix_sums, temp, left, mid)
47        count += divide_and_conquer(prefix_sums, temp, mid + 1, right)
48        count += merge_and_count(prefix_sums, temp, left, mid, right)
49        return count
50
51    # Build prefix sums
52    prefix_sums = [0]
53    for num in nums:
54        prefix_sums.append(prefix_sums[-1] + num)
55
56    temp = [0] * len(prefix_sums)
57    return divide_and_conquer(prefix_sums, temp, 0, len(prefix_sums) - 1)

Template 4: Array Reconstruction

1def create_sorted_array(instructions):
2    """Create sorted array with minimum cost"""
3    def merge_and_count(arr, temp, left, mid, right):
4        smaller_count = [0] * len(arr)
5        larger_count = [0] * len(arr)
6
7        # Count smaller and larger elements during merge
8        i, j = left, mid + 1
9        for k in range(left, right + 1):
10            if i > mid:
11                temp[k] = arr[j]
12                j += 1
13            elif j > right:
14                temp[k] = arr[i]
15                # Count how many elements from right half are smaller
16                smaller_count[arr[i][1]] += (right - mid)
17                i += 1
18            elif arr[i][0] <= arr[j][0]:
19                temp[k] = arr[i]
20                smaller_count[arr[i][1]] += (j - mid - 1)
21                i += 1
22            else:
23                temp[k] = arr[j]
24                j += 1
25
26        # Copy back
27        for k in range(left, right + 1):
28            arr[k] = temp[k]
29
30        return smaller_count, larger_count
31
32    def merge_sort_with_count(arr, temp, left, right):
33        if left >= right:
34            return
35
36        mid = (left + right) // 2
37        merge_sort_with_count(arr, temp, left, mid)
38        merge_sort_with_count(arr, temp, mid + 1, right)
39        merge_and_count(arr, temp, left, mid, right)
40
41    # Implementation depends on specific problem
42    # This is a general framework for array reconstruction
43    pass

Problems by Pattern

Inversion Counting Problems

Problem LC # Key Technique Difficulty
Count of Smaller Numbers After Self 315 Modified merge sort Hard
Reverse Pairs 493 Conditional inversion count Hard
Count of Range Sum 327 Prefix sum + D&C Hard
Create Sorted Array through Instructions 1649 Dynamic inversion counting Hard

Range Query Problems

Problem LC # Key Technique Difficulty
Count of Range Sum 327 Prefix sum merging Hard
Number of Pairs Satisfying Inequality 2426 Range condition D&C Hard

LC Examples

2-1) Count of Smaller Numbers After Self (LC 315) — Merge Sort with Index Tracking

During merge, count how many right-half elements moved past each left-half element.

1// LC 315 - Count of Smaller Numbers After Self
2// IDEA: Merge sort on indices; each right-side element that passes left side increments its count
3// time = O(N log N), space = O(N)
4public List<Integer> countSmaller(int[] nums) {
5    int n = nums.length;
6    int[] counts = new int[n], indices = new int[n];
7    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) indices[i] = i;
8    mergeSort(nums, indices, counts, 0, n - 1);
9    List<Integer> res = new ArrayList<>();
10    for (int c : counts) res.add(c);
11    return res;
12}
13private void mergeSort(int[] nums, int[] idx, int[] counts, int l, int r) {
14    if (l >= r) return;
15    int mid = (l + r) / 2;
16    mergeSort(nums, idx, counts, l, mid);
17    mergeSort(nums, idx, counts, mid + 1, r);
18    int[] tmp = new int[r - l + 1];
19    int i = l, j = mid + 1, k = 0, rightMoved = 0;
20    while (i <= mid && j <= r) {
21        if (nums[idx[j]] < nums[idx[i]]) { tmp[k++] = idx[j++]; rightMoved++; }
22        else { counts[idx[i]] += rightMoved; tmp[k++] = idx[i++]; }
23    }
24    while (i <= mid) { counts[idx[i]] += rightMoved; tmp[k++] = idx[i++]; }
25    while (j <= r) tmp[k++] = idx[j++];
26    System.arraycopy(tmp, 0, idx, l, tmp.length);
27}

1def countSmaller(nums):
2    """Count how many numbers after each element are smaller"""
3    def merge_and_count(indices, temp, left, mid, right):
4        # Count smaller elements to the right
5        i, j, k = left, mid + 1, left
6
7        while i <= mid and j <= right:
8            if nums[indices[i]] <= nums[indices[j]]:
9                temp[k] = indices[i]
10                # All elements from mid+1 to j-1 are smaller than nums[indices[i]]
11                counts[indices[i]] += (j - mid - 1)
12                i += 1
13            else:
14                temp[k] = indices[j]
15                j += 1
16            k += 1
17
18        # Process remaining elements
19        while i <= mid:
20            temp[k] = indices[i]
21            counts[indices[i]] += (j - mid - 1)
22            i += 1
23            k += 1
24        while j <= right:
25            temp[k] = indices[j]
26            j += 1
27            k += 1
28
29        # Copy back
30        for i in range(left, right + 1):
31            indices[i] = temp[i]
32
33    def merge_sort(indices, temp, left, right):
34        if left >= right:
35            return
36
37        mid = (left + right) // 2
38        merge_sort(indices, temp, left, mid)
39        merge_sort(indices, temp, mid + 1, right)
40        merge_and_count(indices, temp, left, mid, right)
41
42    n = len(nums)
43    counts = [0] * n
44    indices = list(range(n))
45    temp = [0] * n
46
47    merge_sort(indices, temp, 0, n - 1)
48    return counts

2-2) Reverse Pairs (LC 493) — Merge Sort Count Before Merge

Count cross-half pairs (nums[i] > 2*nums[j]) with two pointers before doing the merge.

1// LC 493 - Reverse Pairs
2// IDEA: Merge sort — count i>2j pairs across halves first, then merge normally
3// time = O(N log N), space = O(N)
4public int reversePairs(int[] nums) {
5    return mergeSort(nums, 0, nums.length - 1);
6}
7private int mergeSort(int[] nums, int l, int r) {
8    if (l >= r) return 0;
9    int mid = (l + r) / 2;
10    int count = mergeSort(nums, l, mid) + mergeSort(nums, mid + 1, r);
11    int j = mid + 1;
12    for (int i = l; i <= mid; i++) {
13        while (j <= r && nums[i] > 2L * nums[j]) j++;
14        count += j - (mid + 1);
15    }
16    int[] tmp = new int[r - l + 1];
17    int i = l, k = 0; j = mid + 1;
18    while (i <= mid && j <= r) tmp[k++] = nums[i] <= nums[j] ? nums[i++] : nums[j++];
19    while (i <= mid) tmp[k++] = nums[i++];
20    while (j <= r)   tmp[k++] = nums[j++];
21    System.arraycopy(tmp, 0, nums, l, tmp.length);
22    return count;
23}

1def reversePairs(nums):
2    """Count pairs where nums[i] > 2 * nums[j] for i < j"""
3    def merge_and_count(nums, temp, left, mid, right):
4        # Count reverse pairs first
5        count = 0
6        j = mid + 1
7        for i in range(left, mid + 1):
8            while j <= right and nums[i] > 2 * nums[j]:
9                j += 1
10            count += j - (mid + 1)
11
12        # Regular merge
13        i, j, k = left, mid + 1, left
14        while i <= mid and j <= right:
15            if nums[i] <= nums[j]:
16                temp[k] = nums[i]
17                i += 1
18            else:
19                temp[k] = nums[j]
20                j += 1
21            k += 1
22
23        while i <= mid:
24            temp[k] = nums[i]
25            i += 1
26            k += 1
27        while j <= right:
28            temp[k] = nums[j]
29            j += 1
30            k += 1
31
32        for i in range(left, right + 1):
33            nums[i] = temp[i]
34
35        return count
36
37    def merge_sort_and_count(nums, temp, left, right):
38        count = 0
39        if left < right:
40            mid = (left + right) // 2
41            count += merge_sort_and_count(nums, temp, left, mid)
42            count += merge_sort_and_count(nums, temp, mid + 1, right)
43            count += merge_and_count(nums, temp, left, mid, right)
44        return count
45
46    temp = [0] * len(nums)
47    return merge_sort_and_count(nums[:], temp, 0, len(nums) - 1)

2-3) Count of Range Sum (LC 327) — Merge Sort on Prefix Sums

Build prefix sum array; count cross-half pairs (prefixSum[j] - prefixSum[i]) in [lower, upper].

1// LC 327 - Count of Range Sum
2// IDEA: Merge sort on prefix sums; count cross pairs in [lower, upper] before merging
3// time = O(N log N), space = O(N)
4public int countRangeSum(int[] nums, int lower, int upper) {
5    int n = nums.length;
6    long[] prefix = new long[n + 1];
7    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) prefix[i + 1] = prefix[i] + nums[i];
8    return mergeSort(prefix, 0, n, lower, upper);
9}
10private int mergeSort(long[] p, int l, int r, int lo, int hi) {
11    if (l >= r) return 0;
12    int mid = (l + r) / 2;
13    int count = mergeSort(p, l, mid, lo, hi) + mergeSort(p, mid + 1, r, lo, hi);
14    int j = mid + 1, k = mid + 1;
15    for (int i = l; i <= mid; i++) {
16        while (j <= r && p[j] - p[i] < lo) j++;
17        while (k <= r && p[k] - p[i] <= hi) k++;
18        count += k - j;
19    }
20    long[] tmp = new long[r - l + 1];
21    int i = l, a = mid + 1, idx = 0;
22    while (i <= mid && a <= r) tmp[idx++] = p[i] <= p[a] ? p[i++] : p[a++];
23    while (i <= mid) tmp[idx++] = p[i++];
24    while (a <= r)   tmp[idx++] = p[a++];
25    System.arraycopy(tmp, 0, p, l, tmp.length);
26    return count;
27}

1def countRangeSum(nums, lower, upper):
2    """Count subarrays with sum in [lower, upper]"""
3    def merge_and_count(prefix_sums, temp, left, mid, right):
4        count = 0
5        j = k = mid + 1
6
7        for i in range(left, mid + 1):
8            # Find range where prefix_sums[x] - prefix_sums[i] is in [lower, upper]
9            while j <= right and prefix_sums[j] - prefix_sums[i] < lower:
10                j += 1
11            while k <= right and prefix_sums[k] - prefix_sums[i] <= upper:
12                k += 1
13            count += k - j
14
15        # Merge sorted arrays
16        i, j, p = left, mid + 1, left
17        while i <= mid and j <= right:
18            if prefix_sums[i] <= prefix_sums[j]:
19                temp[p] = prefix_sums[i]
20                i += 1
21            else:
22                temp[p] = prefix_sums[j]
23                j += 1
24            p += 1
25
26        while i <= mid:
27            temp[p] = prefix_sums[i]
28            i += 1
29            p += 1
30        while j <= right:
31            temp[p] = prefix_sums[j]
32            j += 1
33            p += 1
34
35        for i in range(left, right + 1):
36            prefix_sums[i] = temp[i]
37
38        return count
39
40    def divide_and_conquer(prefix_sums, temp, left, right):
41        if left >= right:
42            return 0
43
44        mid = (left + right) // 2
45        count = divide_and_conquer(prefix_sums, temp, left, mid)
46        count += divide_and_conquer(prefix_sums, temp, mid + 1, right)
47        count += merge_and_count(prefix_sums, temp, left, mid, right)
48        return count
49
50    # Build prefix sum array
51    prefix_sums = [0]
52    for num in nums:
53        prefix_sums.append(prefix_sums[-1] + num)
54
55    temp = [0] * len(prefix_sums)
56    return divide_and_conquer(prefix_sums, temp, 0, len(prefix_sums) - 1)

Advanced Techniques

Optimization Strategies

1def divide_and_conquer_optimizations():
2    """Various optimization techniques for D&C"""
3
4    # 1. In-place operations to reduce space
5    def in_place_merge(arr, left, mid, right):
6        # Reduce auxiliary space usage
7        pass
8
9    # 2. Iterative bottom-up approach
10    def iterative_merge_sort(arr):
11        n = len(arr)
12        size = 1
13        while size < n:
14            left = 0
15            while left < n - 1:
16                mid = min(left + size - 1, n - 1)
17                right = min(left + 2 * size - 1, n - 1)
18                # merge(arr, left, mid, right)
19                left += 2 * size
20            size *= 2
21
22    # 3. Parallel divide and conquer
23    def parallel_merge_sort(arr):
24        # Use threading for large datasets
25        pass
26
27    # 4. Hybrid approach with insertion sort for small subarrays
28    def hybrid_merge_sort(arr, threshold=10):
29        if len(arr) <= threshold:
30            return insertion_sort(arr)
31        # Regular merge sort for larger arrays

Custom Merge Logic Patterns

1class AdvancedMergePatterns:
2    """Advanced merge logic for specific problems"""
3
4    def merge_with_multiple_conditions(self, arr1, arr2):
5        """Merge with multiple counting conditions"""
6        result = []
7        i = j = 0
8        counts = {"condition1": 0, "condition2": 0}
9
10        while i < len(arr1) and j < len(arr2):
11            if self.condition1(arr1[i], arr2[j]):
12                counts["condition1"] += len(arr2) - j
13            if self.condition2(arr1[i], arr2[j]):
14                counts["condition2"] += len(arr2) - j
15
16            if arr1[i] <= arr2[j]:
17                result.append(arr1[i])
18                i += 1
19            else:
20                result.append(arr2[j])
21                j += 1
22
23        return result + arr1[i:] + arr2[j:], counts
24
25    def merge_with_reconstruction(self, left_part, right_part):
26        """Merge while reconstructing array with specific properties"""
27        # Custom merge logic for array reconstruction problems
28        pass

Performance Optimization Tips

Time Complexity Analysis

1def complexity_analysis():
2    """Analyze time complexity of different D&C approaches"""
3
4    # Standard divide and conquer: T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n) = O(n log n)
5    # With k-way division: T(n) = kT(n/k) + O(n) = O(n log n) if k is constant
6    # With additional work per level: T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n^c)
7    #   - If c < 1: O(n log n)
8    #   - If c = 1: O(n log n)
9    #   - If c > 1: O(n^c)
10
11    pass

Space Optimization

1def space_optimizations():
2    """Techniques to reduce space complexity"""
3
4    # 1. Reuse auxiliary arrays
5    def reuse_temp_array(arr):
6        temp = [0] * len(arr)  # Create once, reuse everywhere
7        # Pass temp to all recursive calls
8
9    # 2. In-place merge (complex but saves space)
10    def in_place_merge_technique(arr, left, mid, right):
11        # Advanced in-place merging algorithms
12        pass
13
14    # 3. Iterative approach to eliminate recursion stack
15    def iterative_divide_conquer(arr):
16        # Bottom-up approach to save stack space
17        pass

Summary & Quick Reference

Common D&C Patterns

Pattern Template Use Case Example
Basic Inversion Modified merge sort Count inversions Simple inversion count
Conditional Count Merge with conditions Specific pair conditions Reverse pairs
Range Queries Prefix sum + D&C Range sum problems Count range sum
Reconstruction Merge with building Array construction Sorted array creation

Time Complexity Guide

Problem Type Time Complexity Space Complexity Notes
Basic Inversion O(n log n) O(n) Standard merge sort
Conditional Inversion O(n log n) O(n) Additional condition checks
Range Sum O(n log n) O(n) Prefix sum preprocessing
Reconstruction O(n log n) O(n) May need additional structures

Common Mistakes & Tips

🚫 Common Mistakes:

  • Forgetting to handle edge cases in merge step
  • Incorrect index management during merge
  • Not preserving original array when needed
  • Inefficient condition checking in merge

✅ Best Practices:

  • Always use auxiliary array for merge operations
  • Handle left and right boundaries carefully
  • Optimize condition checking in merge step
  • Consider iterative approach for very large inputs
  • Use stable sorting when relative order matters

Interview Tips

  1. Identify D&C opportunities: Look for inversion counting, range queries
  2. Master merge logic: The key is in the custom merge step
  3. Handle indices carefully: Off-by-one errors are common
  4. Consider space-time tradeoffs: Auxiliary space vs. in-place operations
  5. Practice merge variations: Different counting/reconstruction logic
  6. Test edge cases: Empty arrays, single elements, duplicate values

This comprehensive divide and conquer cheatsheet covers the most important patterns and techniques for solving complex counting and range query problems efficiently.
